era_context: Continuation of the Iron Age II Levant. Shalmaneser III remains on the Assyrian throne, conducting annual campaigns. The Kingdom of Israel under Ahab continues to resist Assyrian pressure, often in coalition with neighboring states. Phoenician city-states maintain their commercial networks. Egypt remains fragmented. This year falls within the period of intensive Assyrian military activity and Levantine resistance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia (Chinese sources may be underrepresented)

events.title: Shalmaneser III's Seventh Campaign into Syria-Palestine
events.description: Shalmaneser III conducts his seventh documented campaign into the Levant, continuing the pattern of annual military expeditions. The campaign targets the same coalition of Syrian and Palestinian states. These campaigns are part of Shalmaneser III's strategy to establish Assyrian hegemony over the region and extract tribute. The campaigns do not result in permanent territorial conquest but establish a pattern of Assyrian dominance that will intensify in subsequent decades.

sources.name: Assyrian Royal Annals and inscriptions of Shalmaneser III

events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser III's annals document his campaigns, but the precise year-by-year sequence is reconstructed from regnal chronology. The dating is secure within ±1–2 years based on Assyrian king lists and astronomical observations.
cross_references: Part of series of campaigns documented in -844-001, -842-001

disconfirming_evidence: The historical record for 843 BCE is extremely sparse outside of Assyrian sources. No independent confirmation of specific events in this year exists from Levantine, Egyptian, or other sources. The significance of individual campaigns may be inflated in Assyrian propaganda. Some scholars question whether all documented campaigns actually occurred or whether some were duplicated or invented for rhetorical effect.
historiographic_note: This year is almost entirely documented through Assyrian royal inscriptions, which are inherently propagandistic and focused on military achievements. No contemporary sources from the Levant, Egypt, or elsewhere provide independent verification. Archaeological evidence is sparse and difficult to date to this specific year. The record is heavily skewed toward Assyrian military narrative; the perspective of the Levantine states under pressure is almost entirely absent.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same campaign season or immediately following
